1:
The chapter opens on an act of remembered covenant, and the witnesses divide gently over why it comes so late and what prompts it. Keil and Delitzsch set the scene: now exalted king over all Israel, David “sought to show compassion to the house of the fallen king,” repaying the love Jonathan had once sworn before the Lord (1 Samuel 20:13; cf. 2 Samuel 23:17). Keil dates the episode to about the middle of the reign, reasoning that Mephibosheth, only five when Saul died (2 Samuel 4:4), now has a young son (2 Samuel 9:12); the story stands as an appendix to the first section of the history, meant to show David “mindful of the duty of gratitude and loving fidelity” at the very height of his power.

The great question is why David waited so long. Matthew Henry admits plainly that the king “had too long forgotten his obligations to Jonathan,” yet turns the delay into a lesson — it is good to bethink ourselves of neglected promises, “better do it late than never” — and reads the scene through Paul’s epitaph that David “served his generation according to the will of God” (Acts 13:36). Adam Clarke supplies a colder motive: “reasons of state often destroy all the charities of life,” and political calculation, he suspects, kept David from acting sooner. John Wesley offers the kindest reading: David inquires after “the house of Saul,” not the house of Jonathan, because he did not know a son survived; Mephibosheth was young and obscure, “possibly concealed by his friends, lest David should cut him off, as hath been usual among princes.”

Henry lingers on the sort of kindness this is. David does not wait to be petitioned but actively seeks an object for his charity, for “the most necessitous are the least clamorous” and “the liberal deviseth liberal things” (Isaiah 32:8). The wonder is the recipient. Saul had been David’s sworn enemy and his house “a bloody house,” now so emptied that it was a question whether any survived at all (2 Samuel 21:1). Where Abimelech had destroyed the house of Gideon (Judges 9:5) and Athaliah the seed royal (2 Chronicles 22:10), David’s throne “needed no such vile supports.” He shows kindness for Jonathan’s sake, honouring the sworn covenant (1 Samuel 20:42) and Jonathan’s own plea, “show me the kindness of the LORD, that I die not” (1 Samuel 20:14). John Gill and Henry alike hear in “the kindness of God” more than common generosity — kindness measured by covenant and modelled on God’s own mercy, the same charity our religion commands even toward those who have wronged us (Matthew 5:44; 1 Peter 3:9). All agree the scene’s beauty is that gratitude, not policy, moves the king.
2:
The search for a survivor produces a name, and the witnesses attend to the man who supplies it. John Gill identifies Ziba as one who “belonged to Saul’s family” — not that any of Saul’s own blood was a servant, but a retainer of the household. Gill records the Jewish tradition that Ziba was “a Canaanitish servant,” who on his master’s death was not set free but passed as an inheritance to the children, though he notes that Josephus reports the opposite, that Saul had made him free. Either way he is the old steward who has outlived the dynasty he served, the one man positioned to know what became of its scattered remnant. Gill’s interest in Ziba’s legal status is not idle antiquarianism: a bondman who had passed with the estate would still be bound to serve Saul’s heirs, which is why David can presently commission him to farm the land for Mephibosheth, while a freedman’s service would be of another kind. The very question of whether Saul had liberated him or not touches the authority by which the king now disposes of both the man and the fields he tends.

Keil and Delitzsch look ahead to what Ziba’s answer sets in motion: at his word David “sent for this son of Jonathan,” Mephibosheth (cf. 2 Samuel 4:4), and would presently “not only restore his father’s possessions in land, but take him to his own royal table for the rest of his life.” The first word the king will speak to the trembling heir is “Fear not” — Keil already anticipates the dread of a man summoned before the ruler who had displaced his grandfather’s line.

The exchange itself is deliberately formal: “Art thou Ziba? And he said, Thy servant is he.” The commentators pass over it quickly, yet the verse quietly turns the whole account, for it is through this surviving servant that the buried house of Saul is found again. What providence had scattered and obscurity had hidden, a single old servant’s memory now recovers; the king’s charitable purpose of 2 Samuel 9:1 would have died for want of an object had there been no Ziba to answer it. The thinness of the notice matches the thinness of the man’s own place in the story — a hinge, not a hero — but the witnesses let him stand as the instrument by which David’s covenant with Jonathan at last finds its heir. It is worth marking, too, how the narrative dwells on the formality: the king does not simply describe the man he seeks but has him summoned and named to his face. The reader is thereby introduced to a figure who will not stay minor — the same Ziba reappears at the great crises of the reign, first slandering his master (2 Samuel 16:3) and then meeting the returning king with a show of loyalty — so that this quiet first mention plants a character whose fidelity the later chapters will search out.
3:
The king presses his question and now names the measure of the mercy he intends — “the kindness of God” — the phrase on which the witnesses fasten. John Gill glosses the plain sense, that David asks whether any of Saul’s house is “remaining or living.” Adam Clarke draws out the Hebrew idiom in the great phrase: “the kindness of God” means “the utmost, the highest degrees of kindness,” on the same principle by which “the hail of God” is very great hail and “the mountains of God” are exceeding high mountains — a superlative formed by appeal to the divine. This is no ordinary courtesy but kindness raised to its highest pitch. The idiom matters for the whole scene, for it lifts David’s intention above mere royal patronage: he does not propose to do the heir a favour proportioned to a beaten enemy’s grandson, but to lavish on him a mercy answerable to God’s own name. What the king means, on Clarke’s reading, is nothing the recipient could ever have asked or deserved.

Clarke adds a second layer: the kindness is also “according to the covenant of God made between him and the family of Jonathan.” So the phrase looks two ways — upward, to the immeasurable scale of God’s own generosity that David means to imitate, and backward, to the sworn bond of 1 Samuel 20:14-15, where Jonathan had begged, “show me the kindness of the LORD, that I die not; and the same to my seed.” The kindness David offers is thus at once godlike in its greatness and God-witnessed in its origin.

Ziba’s reply narrows the field to one: “Jonathan hath yet a son, which is lame on his feet.” The commentators let the detail stand without much comment, but it is loaded — the heir of Saul’s house is a cripple, disabled since the flight in which his nurse let him fall (2 Samuel 4:4), living far off and forgotten. That the recipient of “the kindness of God” should be a lame and obscure survivor only deepens the pattern the whole chapter draws: mercy sought out, not earned; bestowed on weakness, not merit. David asks for someone to whom he “may show kindness,” and is answered with the neediest object imaginable — which is precisely the sort the liberal heart, as Henry observed on 2 Samuel 9:1, goes looking for. The commentators feel the fitness of it: the “kindness of God” is precisely the kindness that stoops to the helpless, and the one man left of Saul’s house is helpless indeed, unable even to stand before the king without being carried. The greatness of the mercy and the smallness of its object are set deliberately together, so that the verse reads less like a transaction between rulers than like grace descending upon need.
4:
The single verse is a question and its answer — “Where is he?… Behold, he is in the house of Machir, the son of Ammiel, in Lodebar” — and the witnesses treat it as a matter of geography and of the diligence of a king who will not let distance excuse neglect. John Gill notes only that David asks “in what part of the land” the man dwells; the king’s charity is practical, wanting an address, not merely a sentiment. Adam Clarke turns to the place: Lodebar, he says, is “supposed to have been situated beyond Jordan,” though “there is nothing certain known concerning it” — an obscure town on the far side of the river, a fitting hiding-place for a forgotten prince. The uncertainty the commentators confess about the site is itself eloquent: a place so obscure that later scholars cannot fix it on the map is exactly where a hunted heir would be hidden away, far from Jerusalem and far from notice. Gill and Clarke, between the plain question and the doubtful geography, sketch the distance grace must travel to reach its object.

That Mephibosheth is found in the house of Machir the son of Ammiel is itself telling. He is not on his own estate nor at any court, but a dependent in another man’s household beyond the Jordan, in a place whose very name later interpreters could scarcely locate. The obscurity is the point: the last male heir of Israel’s first royal house lives in exile from his inheritance, sheltered by the charity of a stranger. The king who asks “Where is he?” must reach across the Jordan into near-anonymity to find the one he means to bless.

The mention of Machir also plants a seed the narrative will later harvest. The same Machir of Lodebar reappears among those who supply David himself when he flees from Absalom (2 Samuel 17:27), so that the household now sheltering Saul’s grandson will one day shelter David’s own cause. The witnesses, sparing here, let the bare topography carry the weight: a king’s mercy tracing a hidden man to an uncertain town, and providence quietly noting the door through which that mercy runs. The verse asks and answers only “Where is he?”, yet in that short exchange the whole distance between a throne in Jerusalem and a crippled dependent beyond Jordan is measured — and the king shows himself willing to cross it.
5:
David acts on the address at once — “Then king David sent, and fetched him out of the house of Machir” — and the witnesses read in the sending both the king’s promptness and the character of the man who had sheltered the heir. John Gill supposes the messengers may well have included Ziba himself, “none being more proper than he that knew him, and where he was.” The steward who disclosed Mephibosheth’s whereabouts is the natural guide to fetch him, and Gill’s small conjecture keeps the narrative economical: the same man who supplied the address is sent to bring in the heir, so that no time is lost between the king’s resolve and its accomplishment.

John Wesley fixes his attention on Machir, “a generous man, who entertained Mephibosheth out of mere compassion, not of disaffection to David.” This matters, for a great household sheltering the last of Saul’s line might easily be read as harbouring a rival; Wesley clears Machir of any such disloyalty. The proof, he observes, comes later: this same Machir “we find kind to David himself, when he fled from Absalom” (2 Samuel 17:27). Out of that reversal Wesley draws his lesson — “David now little thought that the time would come when he himself should need his assistance”; therefore “let us be forward to give, because we know not what we ourselves may sometime want” (Ecclesiastes 11:2).

The verb “fetched” carries a gentle irony the commentators feel. The grandson of Saul is brought up to Jerusalem not as a captive but as a guest, though he cannot yet know the difference — hence the “Fear not” that will meet him. A king sends royal messengers across the Jordan to a private house to bring in a lame and landless man; the whole machinery of the crown is set in motion for an act of pure grace. What began as David’s inward resolve in 2 Samuel 9:1 now becomes public motion, messengers dispatched and the road travelled, and the forgotten heir is drawn out of Lodebar toward a table he never expected to share. Wesley’s lesson from Machir gives the moment its moral colour: the same providence that once made David a fugitive and now makes him a king will one day make him a fugitive again, and the kindnesses shown along the way are stored up against a day of need. The verse that fetches Mephibosheth is thus, in Wesley’s hands, also a quiet exhortation to open-handedness, since none of us knows on whose charity we may yet depend.
6:
Mephibosheth arrives, and the witnesses mark the depth of his self-abasement. John Gill pauses on the name and lineage — “the son of Jonathan, the son of Saul” — noting that this heir is elsewhere called Meribbaal (1 Chronicles 8:34), and that in him the blood of both the fallen king and David’s beloved friend meets. Keil and Delitzsch describe the meeting: he “fell on his face, and did reverence,” expressing his thanks “with the deepest obeisance, and a confession of his unworthiness of any such favour.” The double name Gill notes is more than a genealogical curiosity: the alternate form Meribbaal, still carrying the name of the Canaanite Baal, belongs to the old house of Saul, while the narrative’s Mephibosheth marks the heir as he now stands before David — the last relic of a rejected line, prostrate before the throne that replaced it.

The gesture answers an old scene. Matthew Henry hears the echo across the years: David had once bowed himself to Jonathan when Jonathan stood next the throne (1 Samuel 20:41), and now Jonathan’s son does homage to David “when affairs are so completely reversed.” Those who show respect in their low estate, Henry adds, shall have respect shown them when they are raised. The reverence of the son repays, in a manner, the reverence the father once received.

Keil links the prostration to the confession Mephibosheth will voice in the next verse — his likening of himself to “a dead dog” (cf. 1 Samuel 24:15) — and notes that David meets the trembling man with “Fear not,” dispelling any anxiety lest the king intend harm. The whole encounter is framed by the crippled man’s terror and the king’s reassurance. When David answers simply by naming him — “Mephibosheth” — and the man replies “Behold thy servant,” the exchange fixes the tone of the chapter: majesty stooping to weakness, and weakness scarcely daring to believe the stoop. Keil observes that David next summons Ziba to settle the estate, so that the personal welcome flows straight into the ordering of the inheritance. The witnesses do not contend over the scene; they read it with one mind as a study in how the great should receive the low. Henry’s point about repaid reverence and Keil’s about dispelled fear meet in the same picture: a king who once did homage to the father now stoops to reassure the son, and a son who has every reason to expect a sentence hears instead his own name spoken in welcome. The verse turns entirely on that reversal, and the commentators let its quiet drama stand.
7:
This is the verse of the great promise — “Fear not… I will surely shew thee kindness for Jonathan thy father’s sake, and will restore thee all the land of Saul thy father; and thou shalt eat bread at my table continually” — and the witnesses distinguish carefully between its two gifts. John Gill accounts for the “Fear not”: David “might observe a dejection in his countenance, a trembling in his limbs,” the natural dread of one “of the seed royal” that a reigning king would cut him off. The reassurance is aimed at a real terror.

Adam Clarke draws the sharp legal line that governs the verse. The restored land, he argues, is “the mere family estate of the house of Kish,” which David as king “might have retained,” but which “most certainly belonged, according to the Israelitish law, to the descendants of the family.” To give it back, then, was justice, not favour. The kindness lies wholly in the second gift: “thou shalt eat bread at my table” was “the highest honour that any subject could enjoy.” Clarke reaches for the Lord’s own words to measure it, hearing an echo in the promise to the disciples “that ye may eat and drink at my table in my kingdom” (Luke 22:30). For a man in Mephibosheth’s condition, Clarke adds, David “could do no more,” since “his lameness rendered him unfit for any public employment”; the table is the whole of his portion of honour.

The ground of both gifts is named twice over — “for Jonathan thy father’s sake.” Gill and Clarke agree that nothing in Mephibosheth commends him: he is lame, landless, and afraid, the heir of a rejected house. What he receives, he receives on another’s account, the covenant love owed to his father reaching one who could claim nothing for himself. The promise of a perpetual seat at the royal table — “continually” — turns a summons that might have meant death into adoption into the king’s household. Henry’s later reflection that David here images Christ’s welcome of fallen men (drawn out at 2 Samuel 9:9) has its root in this verse: unearned restoration, and a place at the table, granted for the sake of another.
8:
Mephibosheth’s answer is a single stroke of self-abasement — “What is thy servant, that thou shouldest look upon such a dead dog as I am?” — and the witnesses read it as the fitting posture of one lifted by pure grace. John Gill takes the bow as a sign “of gratitude, and… of humility, and of the sense he had of his unworthiness to enjoy such a favour.” The man does not receive the king’s bounty as his due but as an astonishment. The image he chooses is the lowest a Hebrew tongue could reach: a dog was already an unclean and contemptible creature, and a dead dog the very refuse of contempt, so that Mephibosheth ranks himself beneath the meanest thing alive. That a grandson of the king should reach for such a word is the measure of how completely his family’s fall has emptied him of any claim.

Matthew Henry marvels at how the heir “vilifies himself.” Though “the son of a prince, and the grandson of a king,” yet his family being “under guilt and wrath, and himself poor and lame, he calls himself a dead dog before David” — much as David had once asked “who am I?” (1 Samuel 18:18). Henry sets this against the ingratitude of those who “take every favour as a debt, and think every thing too little that their friends do for them”; Mephibosheth, on the contrary, reckons himself “less than the least of all his favours,” magnifying the kindness he might so easily have belittled.

John Wesley draws the lesson the scene presses: “it is good to have the heart humbled under humbling providences.” When “divine providence brings our condition down,” and “divine grace brings our spirits down” with it, then, Wesley says, “we shall be easy.” Henry adds the promise that crowns such lowliness — “those who thus humble themselves shall be exalted.” The witnesses find in the crippled man’s word a model of how grace should be met: not with a sense of entitlement, but with wonder that the king should look at all upon a dead dog. There is no contention among them; together they make Mephibosheth’s lowliness the mirror of every soul that receives an unearned mercy. The very posture the verse records — a bow, and a word of self-abasement — is what fits a man to be blessed, for the heart that owns itself a dead dog is the heart most ready to be astonished at kindness. So the witnesses turn the crippled prince’s confession into a pattern: grace is best met on the face, and the deepest gratitude springs from the lowest estimate of self.
9:
With the heir welcomed, the king turns to provision, and the witnesses gather around the practical settlement and the great typology Henry draws from it. Keil and Delitzsch explain the arrangement: David summons Ziba, tells him “of the restoration of Saul’s possessions to his son Mephibosheth,” and orders him, “with his sons and servants, to cultivate the land.” Keil resolves the apparent tension between “that thy master’s son may have food to eat” and “Mephibosheth shall eat bread alway at my table”: bread is “a general expression,” and though Mephibosheth dined daily with the king, “he had to make provision as a royal prince for the maintenance of his own family and servants,” having children (2 Samuel 9:12; 1 Chronicles 8:34).

Matthew Henry notes the estate must have been large, for Saul’s father was “a mighty man of substance” (1 Samuel 9:1) with “fields and vineyards” to bestow (1 Samuel 22:7), and all of it is now confirmed to Mephibosheth with Ziba called as witness. The management goes to the man who knows the land, though Henry eyes Ziba warily: with fifteen sons and twenty servants to feed from the revenue, “as goods are increased, those are increased that eat them” (Ecclesiastes 5:11), and he recalls the proverb “he that multiplies servants multiplies thieves,” foreseeing how unfaithful Ziba will prove (2 Samuel 16:3). Adam Clarke adds a textual nicety, that the produce in fact supported the young Micha, since the father was “provided for at the table of David,” while “all the patrimony belonged to Mephibosheth.”

Here Henry lifts the whole scene to its highest key. Because David “was a type of Christ,” his kindness to Mephibosheth “illustrates the kindness and love of God our Saviour towards fallen man” — with this difference, that Christ was under no prior obligation as David was to Jonathan. Man, “convicted of rebellion against God,” like Saul’s house lay under sentence, “not only brought low and impoverished, but lame and impotent, made so by the fall.” The Son of God “enquires after this degenerate race, that enquired not after him,” comes “to seek and save them,” and to those who humble themselves “restores the forfeited inheritance,” entitling them “to a better paradise than that which Adam lost,” and takes them “into communion with himself,” setting them “with his children at his table.” Henry can only close in wonder: “Lord, what is man, that thou shouldst thus magnify him!” The witnesses let the domestic act of a grateful king open into the gospel it foreshadows — grace seeking out the lame and the ruined, and seating them at the King’s own board.
10:
The verse spells out the terms of the stewardship — “Thou therefore, and thy sons, and thy servants, shall till the land for him, and thou shalt bring in the fruits… but Mephibosheth thy master’s son shall eat bread alway at my table” — and the witnesses work out the economics. Keil and Delitzsch note the scale of the labour force: “now Ziba had fifteen sons and twenty servants,” a household large enough to farm a considerable estate, whom Keil supposes had been living at Gibeah and perhaps already farming Saul’s land. The detail is not incidental: a man with so numerous a household of his own has both the means to work the whole inheritance and, as the commentators will note, a personal stake in what it yields — which is why the writer troubles to record his fifteen sons and twenty servants at just this point.

Adam Clarke describes the practical relation. Ziba and his family “had the care of the whole estate, and cultivated it at their own expense, yielding the half of the produce to the family of Mephibosheth.” Ziba, he says, “was properly the hind” — the tenant-farmer — “whose duty and interest it was to take proper care of the ground, for the better it was cultivated the more it produced; and his half would consequently be the greater.” Self-interest and duty here run together, which is why Clarke, like Henry, watches the man closely.

The verse holds two provisions in one frame, and the witnesses keep them distinct. Mephibosheth’s honour is personal — a perpetual seat “at my table,” among the king’s own household — while his maintenance as the head of a family runs through the land Ziba tills. John Gill simply marks that Ziba’s sons and servants “were many,” the very abundance that will make the estate productive and, as Henry feared, tempting. The lame prince is thus doubly secured: fed at the king’s board and endowed with his grandfather’s fields, wanting nothing, yet dependent throughout on the fidelity of the steward set over him — a dependence the later story (2 Samuel 16:3) will put sharply to the test. The arrangement is generous and, on its face, complete: the king keeps the man, the steward keeps the land, and the produce flows to the prince’s house. Yet the witnesses cannot read it without a backward glance from the later chapters, where the very steward here entrusted will turn the estate to his own advantage. For now, though, the verse simply seals the provision, and Mephibosheth is left, in Henry’s phrase, easy — with a good estate and no care of it, his living secured by another’s labour and his honour by the king’s table.
11:
Ziba’s compliance — “According to all that my lord the king hath commanded his servant, so shall thy servant do” — sounds like model obedience, but the witnesses hear an irony in it. John Gill notes that Ziba “promised very fair, had he been as faithful to his trust”; the words are dutiful, the heart another matter. Keil and Delitzsch add a grammatical observation on the closing clause, “he shall eat at my table, as one of the king’s sons,” judging the words to be David’s own, gathered up by the writer as he brings the account to its close.

Adam Clarke presses the point hardest, reading the verse against the sequel. Ziba’s “promises were fair and specious, but he was a traitor in his heart, as we shall see in the rebellion of Absalom” (2 Samuel 16:3), when he will slander his master to seize the estate. That David trusted him, Clarke calls “a blot in his character” — yet an excusable one, “for at this time he suspected no evil.” From this Clarke draws a shrewd maxim about the hiddenness of the heart: “circumstances alone can develope the human character. The internal villain can be known only when circumstances occur which can call his propensities into action; till then he may be reputed an honest man.”

The witnesses thus read the verse on two levels at once. On its surface it is the smooth conclusion of a generous settlement — the steward bows, the terms are accepted, the lame prince is provided for “as one of the king’s sons.” Beneath it runs the warning that fair words are no proof of a faithful heart, and that the very arrangement now sealed will one day be abused. Mephibosheth is content with his portion, Ziba pleased with his charge; but the reader, guided by Clarke and Gill, is taught to hold the promise lightly, knowing that only trial will show what such a servant is truly made of. There is a lesson here for more than one court, Clarke implies: that a man may be reputed honest simply because no occasion has yet drawn out his real disposition, and that even a king as discerning as David could be deceived by a fair profession. The verse that appears to tie off the story neatly is, on the witnesses’ reading, the first faint knot of a thread the narrative will later pull.
12:
A single genealogical notice — “and Mephibosheth had a young son, whose name was Micha” — closes the circle of the story, and the witnesses read it as more than a footnote. John Gill observes that though the boy’s age is not given, from this Micha “sprung a progeny of several generations,” the line preserved and multiplied in the very heir who had seemed a dead branch of a ruined house (1 Chronicles 8:34-40). The house of Saul, which the chapter opened by wondering whether any survived, is shown here not merely surviving but continuing. Gill’s glance forward to the long line that would spring from this child gives the notice its quiet force: what looked at the chapter’s outset like the last flicker of a dying house proves instead a root with a future, the name of Saul carried on not through power but through the mercy of the man who displaced him.

The verse adds that “all that dwelt in the house of Ziba were servants unto Mephibosheth.” Keil and Delitzsch take it plainly, that Ziba’s whole establishment now labours for the restored prince. Henry, reflecting on the same clause at 2 Samuel 9:9, had wryly noted how such a train of dependants “lived upon him” and made a prey of the estate; here the writer simply records the fact, that the lame man at the king’s table has become, on paper, the master of a full household.

The little verse quietly answers the chapter’s opening anxiety. David had asked whether anything was “left of the house of Saul” (2 Samuel 9:1); the reply had been a single lame survivor; and now that survivor has a son, a servant-filled house, and a future. Providence, which had emptied Saul’s once-numerous family, does not leave it extinct but preserves — through David’s kindness — the very seed that grace had sought out. The witnesses let the birth of Micha stand as the token that the covenant with Jonathan bore fruit beyond the single generation David set out to bless. The witnesses do not divide over so plain a notice; they simply let it complete the arc. A house emptied by sin and war, reduced to one lame fugitive, is here restored to a table, an estate, a household, and an heir — every loss of the opening verses answered in turn. What the king began out of covenant love, God confirms out of providence, and the chapter that asked whether any of Saul’s house was left can close upon a young son playing in the household of the very prince it feared was the last of his line.
13:
The chapter ends where it aimed — “so Mephibosheth dwelt in Jerusalem: for he did eat continually at the king’s table; and was lame on both his feet” — and the witnesses read the closing line as a deliberate summary. John Gill notes that the heir now “dwelt in Jerusalem,” lodged “in some apartments in the king’s palace, or in some house in the city,” and returned no more to Lodebar nor even to the estate of Saul restored to him; his home is henceforth beside the king. The exile of the opening verses is over.

Adam Clarke reads the verse’s frankness about the man’s condition as its own tribute: “he was fit for no public office,” yet “was treated by the king with the utmost respect and affection.” The narrator’s twice-told note that Mephibosheth “was lame on both his feet” is not cruelty but emphasis — the writer will not let the reader forget that the man so honoured could contribute nothing, that his whole standing rested on grace. The permanent disability and the permanent place at the table are set side by side on purpose. Gill’s note that Mephibosheth never returned to Lodebar nor to his own recovered fields deepens the same point: with a table at the palace open to him continually, the estate becomes a thing to be lived on rather than lived in, and the man himself is drawn permanently into the orbit of the king. His true home is no longer his inheritance but the presence of the one who showed him kindness.

Here the witnesses gather to the point Matthew Henry had drawn out at 2 Samuel 9:9: the lame man kept continually at the king’s board is the picture of fallen men brought near. He is crippled and unprofitable, dwelling by pure favour where he could never have come by right, fed daily at a table he did not earn, for the sake of a covenant made by another. The chapter that began with a king seeking someone to bless ends with the blessed man seated, secure, and lame — a living emblem, as the commentators love to say, of the kindness of God shown to those who can only call themselves dead dogs, and are yet made to eat as the king’s own sons.
